# stream-fork — full reference

`stream-fork` is a toolkit of 1→N stream combinators. It exports three primitives that take an array of downstream sinks and return a single sink distributing incoming chunks across them under a particular dispatch shape, plus a small set of picker helpers under `utils/` for the common `route` shapes. Available in two flavors that share the same API surface but wrap different stream APIs: **Node Streams** (the default `stream-fork` entry, wraps `Writable`) and **Web Streams** (`stream-fork/web`, wraps `WritableStream`).

This file is the long-form LLM-consumable reference. For the project's architectural overview see [ARCHITECTURE.md](./ARCHITECTURE.md); for AI-agent rules see [AGENTS.md](./AGENTS.md); for an end-user-facing summary see [llms.txt](./llms.txt) and the [README](./README.md).

## Why this package exists

Neither Node Streams nor Web Streams ships a built-in primitive for backpressure-preserving fan-out to N outputs:

- Node: `Readable.pipe(dest)` is 1:1. Calling `outputs.forEach(o => o.write(chunk))` and ignoring the callbacks discards backpressure entirely; the slowest downstream's internal buffer grows unboundedly.
- Web: `ReadableStream.tee()` produces two new ReadableStreams, but each branch buffers independently — a slow consumer on one branch causes that branch's queue to grow without limit, while the other branch sees no backpressure signal at all.

`stream-fork` fills both gaps. Each primitive is itself a sink (Writable on Node, WritableStream on Web), so it composes anywhere a sink is expected. Its write callback runs `Promise.all` over the receiving downstreams' write completions, so it only fires once every receiving downstream has acknowledged consumption — the slowest receiver gates upstream.

Three primitives cover the three useful 1→N control-flow shapes:

- **`fork`** — every chunk to every output (broadcast).
- **`route`** — every chunk to exactly one output (single-target dispatch).
- **`filter`** — every chunk to a predicate-selected subset (multi-target conditional).

## Installation

```bash
npm i stream-fork
```

Node 22+ for the Node Streams flavor. Modern browsers / Deno 2+ / Bun 1+ for the Web Streams flavor (anything with a `WritableStream` global).

## The two flavors

The Node and Web flavors ship the same three primitives with the same options surface. The differences:

| | Node (`stream-fork`) | Web (`stream-fork/web`) |
|---|---|---|
| Input array | `Writable[]` | `WritableStream[]` |
| Return type | `Writable` | `WritableStream` |
| Driven by | `source.pipe(forkSink)` | `await source.pipeTo(forkSink)` |
| Picker signature | `(chunk, encoding) => …` | `(chunk) => …` |
| Predicate signature | `(chunk, encoding) => …` | `(chunk) => …` |
| Error surfacing (default) | `'error'` event on the sink | upstream `pipeTo` rejects |
| Pre-write error re-emission | yes (Node only) | n/a (Web has no event model) |
| `options.objectMode` | yes, default `true` | n/a (Web carries any value) |
| `options.queuingStrategy` | n/a | optional, forwarded to `WritableStream` |
| `.outputs` getter | yes | yes |
| `.isEmpty()` method | yes | yes |
| `options.ignoreErrors` | yes | yes |

Picker helpers under `stream-fork/utils/*` are shared between both trees — pure functions with no runtime imports.

## The three primitives

### `fork(outputs[, options])`

Broadcast. Every chunk is forwarded to every live downstream concurrently via `Promise.all` over the pushers' `push(chunk)` calls. The promise resolves when every downstream has acknowledged the write; only then does `fork`'s own write callback fire and upstream resume.

- `outputs` (required) — array of downstream sinks. May be empty (`isEmpty()` will report `true` and the fork is effectively a sink).
- `options` (optional) — Writable options on Node, `{ignoreErrors, queuingStrategy}` on Web.
  - `options.ignoreErrors` — when truthy, downstream errors are silently swallowed and the failing stream is removed from the live set.

Returns a sink with:

- `.outputs` — read-only snapshot of currently-live downstream sinks.
- `.isEmpty()` — `true` when every downstream has failed.

```js
// Node
import fork from 'stream-fork';
const f = fork([sinkA, sinkB, sinkC]);
source.pipe(f);

// Web
import fork from 'stream-fork/web';
await source.pipeTo(fork([sinkA, sinkB, sinkC]));
```

### `route(outputs, options)`

Per-chunk single-target dispatch. For each incoming chunk, `options.pick(chunk[, encoding])` returns the index of the output to forward to; the picked output's write callback gates upstream. Returning a non-index value (or pointing at a dead slot) drops the chunk silently.

- `outputs` (required) — non-empty array of downstream sinks.
- `options` (required) — must include `pick`.
  - `options.pick(chunk[, encoding]) => number | undefined | null` — required picker. (`encoding` is passed only on Node.)
  - `options.ignoreErrors` — same semantics as `fork`.

Returns a sink with the same `.outputs` and `.isEmpty()` surface as `fork`.

```js
import route from 'stream-fork/route.js';

const r = route([evenSink, oddSink], {
  pick: chunk => (chunk % 2 === 0 ? 0 : 1)
});
source.pipe(r);
```

### `filter(outputs, options)`

Per-chunk subset broadcast. `options.predicates` is an array of predicates, one per output. For each incoming chunk, every output whose predicate returns truthy receives the chunk; the slowest of the selected subset gates upstream. An all-true mask is equivalent to `fork`; an all-false mask drops the chunk.

- `outputs` (required) — non-empty array of downstream sinks.
- `options` (required) — must include `predicates` of the same length as `outputs`.
  - `options.predicates: Array<(chunk[, encoding]) => boolean>` — one predicate per output.
  - `options.ignoreErrors` — same semantics as `fork`.

Returns a sink with the same `.outputs` and `.isEmpty()` surface as `fork`.

```js
import filter from 'stream-fork/filter.js';

const f = filter([auditSink, errorSink, allSink], {
  predicates: [log => log.audit, log => log.level === 'error', () => true]
});
source.pipe(f);
```

## Picker helpers

```js
import pickRoundRobin from 'stream-fork/utils/pick-round-robin.js';
import pickByHash from 'stream-fork/utils/pick-by-hash.js';
import pickByKey from 'stream-fork/utils/pick-by-key.js';
import pickFirstMatch from 'stream-fork/utils/pick-first-match.js';
```

All four return a `pick` function suitable for `route()`'s `options.pick`. They are pure functions, runtime-neutral, and identical across Node and Web (work as-is in browsers).

### `pickRoundRobin(count)`

Stateful counter. Returns a function that cycles `0, 1, …, count-1` on each call. Pair with `route` for load-balancing across N parallel workers.

```js
const pick = pickRoundRobin(3);
pick(); // 0
pick(); // 1
pick(); // 2
pick(); // 0
```

### `pickByHash(keyFn, count)`

Returns a picker that maps each chunk to a stable index via `hash(keyFn(chunk)) % count`. The same key always lands on the same output. Numeric keys are used directly modulo `count`; everything else is stringified and djb2-hashed.

```js
const pick = pickByHash(record => record.userId, 4);
// All records with the same userId go to the same shard.
```

### `pickByKey(keyFn, table)`

Returns a picker that looks up `keyFn(chunk)` in `table` (plain object or `Map`) and returns the mapped index, or `undefined` to drop the chunk if the key is missing.

```js
const pick = pickByKey(c => c.tier, {gold: 0, silver: 1, bronze: 2});
pick({tier: 'gold'});    // 0
pick({tier: 'unknown'}); // undefined → chunk dropped
```

### `pickFirstMatch(predicates)`

Returns a picker that tries each predicate in order and returns the index of the first one to match, or `undefined` if none match. Append `() => true` to make a catch-all.

```js
const pick = pickFirstMatch([
  log => log.level === 'error',
  log => log.level === 'warn',
  () => true
]);
```

## Backpressure mechanics

Both Node and Web sinks expose a per-chunk write callback that the upstream driver awaits before producing the next chunk. Each primitive participates in this contract by NOT calling its own write callback until **every receiving downstream** has consumed the chunk. The receiving set is:

- **`fork`** — every live output.
- **`route`** — the single picked output (or none, if the picker dropped).
- **`filter`** — every output whose predicate matched (zero or more).

### Node side

`Writable.write(chunk, encoding, callback)` invokes `callback` when the chunk has been consumed. The per-pusher `push(chunk, encoding)` resolves when the inner `stream.write` callback fires. `Promise.all` over those promises gates the primitive's `_write` callback. The slowest of the receiving downstreams gates the upstream pipe.

The same shape applies to `_final`: every primitive calls `end()` on every pusher and waits for all to acknowledge.

### Web side

`writer.write(chunk)` returns a promise that resolves when the chunk has been accepted into the underlying sink's queue (with sufficient room). The per-pusher `push(chunk)` first awaits `writer.ready` (which resolves when the writer's desired size is > 0), then awaits `writer.write(chunk)`. `Promise.all` over those promises gates our sink's `write` callback. Upstream `pipeTo` sees the deferred resolution as backpressure.

End-of-stream calls `writer.close()` on every pusher. The Web `WritableStream`'s `close()` method on our underlying sink is invoked when upstream ends; we await all per-pusher closes before resolving.

## Error handling

Two modes, controlled by `options.ignoreErrors`.

### Default mode (`!ignoreErrors`)

Errors can arrive through several channels, all unified by the per-flavor pusher.

**Node:**
1. **Downstream emits `'error'`.** The pusher's own `'error'` listener captures the error and marks the pusher dead. The primitive's pre-write listener (installed in default mode only) re-emits the error on the primitive itself if writing hasn't started yet.
2. **Downstream's `write` callback reports an error.** The per-push promise resolves with the error; `_write` finds the first error in its round and forwards it to its own callback (which surfaces as `'error'` on the primitive via the Writable contract).
3. **`stream.write` or `stream.end` throws synchronously.** Same as (2) — the pusher catches the throw and resolves the per-push promise with the thrown error.

**Web:**
1. **Downstream's `writer.closed` rejects.** The pusher's async catch-handler captures the reason and marks the pusher dead.
2. **`writer.write(chunk)` or `writer.close()` rejects.** The per-push / per-end promise resolves with the rejection reason.
3. **`stream.getWriter()` throws** (e.g., the WritableStream is already locked or in an errored state). The pusher is dead before any write attempt.

In both flavors, the failing downstream is marked dead; subsequent writes filter live pushers and skip dead ones. The first error in a round wins:
- Node: surfaces as `'error'` on the primitive.
- Web: rethrown from our underlying sink's `write`, which rejects upstream's `pipeTo` promise.

### Ignore mode (`ignoreErrors: true`)

The pre-write re-emission listener (Node only) is not installed; the per-flavor pusher still catches and marks dead. When a per-push promise resolves with an error, the round-handler returns without rethrow — the error is swallowed. The failing downstream is filtered out and subsequent writes skip it.

## The internal pushers

Each flavor has its own pusher with the same `{push, end, isDead, stream}` shape. **Neither is exported publicly** — the contract may change between minor releases.

### Node: `src/stream-pusher.js`

```js
const makeStreamPusher = stream => ({
  push: (chunk, encoding) => Promise<Error | null>,
  end:  () => Promise<Error | null>,
  isDead: () => boolean,
  stream // the wrapped Writable
});
```

Installs its own `'error'` listener (so Node never crashes on an otherwise-unhandled error) and tracks "dead" state — once any error has been observed via callback, sync throw, or the `'error'` event, subsequent `push`/`end` calls resolve immediately with the stored error.

### Web: `src/web/web-stream-pusher.js`

```js
const makeWebStreamPusher = stream => ({
  push: (chunk) => Promise<Error | null>,
  end:  () => Promise<Error | null>,
  isDead: () => boolean,
  stream // the wrapped WritableStream
});
```

Acquires a writer once via `stream.getWriter()`; awaits `writer.ready` before each `writer.write(chunk)`; listens on `writer.closed` for async errors. `push` and `end` catch all rejections and resolve to `Error | null` rather than rethrowing — the primitives can resume after a downstream fails.

Both pushers factor out per-stream concerns that would otherwise be duplicated across the three primitives.

## Composition with stream-chain

The Node primitives return plain Writables, so each composes naturally as the **last** element of a `stream-chain` pipeline:

```js
import chain from 'stream-chain';
import fork from 'stream-fork';

chain([source, transformA, transformB, fork([sinkA, sinkB])]);
```

`stream-fork` does NOT depend on `stream-chain` — both packages compose at the public stream interface, no runtime cross-dep.

For Web Streams, compose directly with `pipeThrough` / `pipeTo`:

```js
import fork from 'stream-fork/web';

await source
  .pipeThrough(transformA)
  .pipeThrough(transformB)
  .pipeTo(fork([sinkA, sinkB]));
```

## Object mode vs chunk mode (Node only)

The default options literal for the Node flavor is `{objectMode: true}`. To use any Node primitive in chunk mode (Buffer/string), pass an explicit empty options object:

```js
fork([sinkA, sinkB]);       // object mode (default)
fork([sinkA, sinkB], {});   // chunk mode (Buffer/string)
```

Or pass an explicit `objectMode` value:

```js
fork([sinkA, sinkB], {objectMode: false});  // chunk mode
fork([sinkA, sinkB], {objectMode: true});   // object mode
```

The chosen mode is propagated to the primitive's own Writable contract via the super-constructor; the downstream streams' modes must be compatible.

The Web flavor has no analog — Web Streams accept any value, so there is no separate "object mode".

## Notable changes in 3.x

- **Breaking:** ESM-only (`"type": "module"`). Consumers must use `import` (or `await import()` from a CommonJS host).
- **New:** Web Streams flavor at `stream-fork/web` — the same three primitives, wrapping `WritableStream`. Effectively a backpressure-preserving generalization of `ReadableStream.tee()` to N outputs.
- **New internal `makeWebStreamPusher`** mirrors `makeStreamPusher` for the Web side.
- **Tests restructured:** `tests/node/` + `tests/web/`. Browser test workflow via `tape-six-playwright` (`npm run test:browser`).
- **Default-export + named mirror** applied to every public module: every `export default X` is paired with `export {X}`. Picker helpers gain named exports without changing the default-export shape.
- Picker helpers under `stream-fork/utils/*` are unchanged in API and are now shared between flavors.

## Notable changes in 2.x

- **Breaking:** functional API. `new Fork(outputs, options)` from 1.x becomes `fork(outputs, options)` — no `new`, no class. The static `Fork.fork(...)` factory is gone (the function IS the factory).
- **New primitives:** `route` (per-chunk dispatch) and `filter` (subset broadcast) added alongside `fork`.
- **New picker helpers** under `utils/`: `pickRoundRobin`, `pickByHash`, `pickByKey`, `pickFirstMatch`.
- **Node 22+** required.
- **Repackaged** under `src/` with hand-written `.d.ts` sidecars and subpath exports.
- **Test runner** moved from `heya-unit` to `tape-six`.
- **Internal `stream-pusher`** factors per-stream concerns out of each primitive.

## Links

- Repository: https://github.com/uhop/stream-fork
- npm: https://www.npmjs.com/package/stream-fork
- AGENTS.md: agent-facing rules
- ARCHITECTURE.md: design overview
